title: Why your appetite disappears completely during activation
author: Kalesh

metaDescription:

htmlContent:
Your stomach drops and suddenly food is the last thing you want

You know the feeling. You're sitting down to eat and something happens. A text from your boss. A memory you didn't invite. A sound that makes your chest tighten. And just like that, your appetite evaporates. The food in front of you might as well be cardboard. You can't swallow. Your throat feels tight. Your stomach is in knots.

I've been there more times than I can count. For years I thought something was wrong with me. Why couldn't I just eat like a normal person? Why did my body rebel against the most basic act of nourishment the second I felt unsafe?

Here's the thing. This isn't a character flaw. It's not a lack of willpower. It's not even really about food. What you're experiencing is your nervous system doing exactly what it evolved to do. And understanding that can change everything.

When your nervous system detects a threat, real or perceived, it shifts into a state of activation. This could be fight or flight. It could be freeze. Your body is now prioritizing survival over digestion. Think about it. If a tiger is chasing you, do you really need to be digesting your lunch? Of course not. Your body knows this. So it shuts down non-essential functions. And digestion is one of the first things to go.

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, explains this beautifully. Your vagus nerve, which runs from your brainstem to your abdomen, is the main highway for your nervous system's signals. When you're in a state of safety, the ventral vagal branch is active. This allows for what Porges calls "social engagement" and normal digestive function. But when danger is detected, the sympathetic nervous system takes over. Blood flow is redirected away from your stomach and toward your muscles. Saliva production decreases. Gut motility slows down. Your appetite doesn't just disappear. It gets actively suppressed.

This is why you can't force yourself to eat when you're activated. It's not a mental block. It's a biological reality. Your body is literally incapable of digesting food properly in that state. Wild, right?

The freeze response and the complete shutdown of appetite

But there's another layer to this. And it's one that doesn't get talked about enough. Not all activation looks like panic or rage. Sometimes it looks like collapse. Sometimes it looks like nothing at all.

When your nervous system decides that fight or flight isn't going to work, it flips to the dorsal vagal state. This is the freeze response. It's the oldest part of your nervous system, the one we share with reptiles. In this state, your body goes into conservation mode. Your heart rate drops. Your breathing gets shallow. Your metabolism slows down. And your appetite? It doesn't just disappear. It gets buried so deep you might not feel hunger for days.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, has written extensively about how trauma gets stored in the body. He talks about how the freeze response is actually a biological strategy. In the wild, an animal that freezes when caught by a predator might be released if the predator thinks it's already dead. The animal then shakes off the excess energy and goes back to normal. But humans don't do that. We stay frozen. We hold the activation in our tissues. And our digestive system stays locked in that survival mode.

I remember a period in my life when I lost fifteen pounds in three weeks. I wasn't trying to lose weight. I was just so deeply frozen that food felt irrelevant. My body had decided that eating was a luxury I couldn't afford. Looking back, I can see that I was in a chronic dorsal vagal state. I was surviving, not living. And my appetite was the first casualty.

If you've experienced this, you know how confusing it can be. You know you need to eat. You might even want to eat. But your body won't cooperate. The thought of food makes you nauseous. Or you take one bite and feel like you're going to throw up. Or you just don't feel hungry at all, even if it's been hours or days.

This is your nervous system trying to protect you. It's not broken. It's doing its job. But the job it's doing is making it very hard to take care of yourself.

How to gently coax your nervous system back to safety

So what do you do when your appetite is gone and your body is screaming that food is dangerous? The answer isn't to force yourself to eat. That will only reinforce the message that your body isn't safe. The answer is to work with your nervous system, not against it.

The first step is orientation. This is a term from Somatic Experiencing. It means looking around your environment and finding something that feels safe. It could be a color you like. A plant. A pet. A photo. Anything that signals to your nervous system that you are not in immediate danger. Take your time with this. Let your eyes rest on that thing. Notice what happens in your body. Maybe your shoulders drop a little. Maybe your breath gets a tiny bit deeper. That's your nervous system starting to shift.

The second step is grounding. Feel your feet on the floor. Feel your back against the chair. Press your palms together. Squeeze your own arm. These physical sensations can help bring you back into your body and out of the survival state. A tool that many find helpful for releasing stored body tension is TriggerPoint GRID Foam Roller (paid link). Using it gently on your back or legs can release some of the tension that's keeping your nervous system locked in activation. Just go slow. Listen to your body. Don't push into pain.

The third step is small, small, small. Don't try to eat a full meal. Start with something tiny. A cracker. A sip of broth. A single bite of apple. The goal isn't nutrition. The goal is to send a signal to your nervous system that food is safe. When you take that tiny bite, notice what happens. Does your body relax even a millimeter? Does your stomach unclench just a little? That's the feedback loop you're looking for.

Something worth exploring for those drawn to body-based healing is In an Unspoken Voice (paid link) by Peter Levine. This book goes deep into how trauma is held in the body and how you can gently release it. It's not a quick fix. But it's a roadmap for understanding why your body does what it does.

The role of the vagus nerve in restoring digestive function

Your vagus nerve is the key to all of this. It's the communication line between your brain and your gut. When you're in a state of activation, the vagus nerve is inhibited. When you're in a state of safety, it's active. And an active vagus nerve means better digestion, more saliva, more stomach acid, more peristalsis. Everything works the way it's supposed to.

Deb Dana, a clinician who specializes in Polyvagal Theory, talks about "glimmers." These are small moments of safety that your nervous system can latch onto. A warm cup of tea. The sound of rain. A deep breath. A hand on your heart. These glimmers activate your vagus nerve and help bring you back to ventral vagal state. The more you practice noticing glimmers, the more your nervous system learns that safety is possible.

Here's a practice that has helped me. Before I eat, I take three slow breaths. I put my hand on my belly. I look at the food and remind myself that this is nourishment, not a threat. I might even say out loud, "I am safe. This food is safe." It feels silly at first. But your nervous system doesn't understand words. It understands tone and intention. Speaking in a calm, slow voice signals safety to your body in a way that forcing yourself to chew doesn't.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link) by Deb Dana. It's full of simple exercises you can do to map your nervous system and find your own glimmers. It's not about fixing yourself. It's about learning to befriend the system that's been trying to protect you all along.

Another tool that can help is gentle pressure on your body. A YnM Weighted Blanket 15 lbs (paid link) can provide the kind of deep touch pressure that calms the nervous system. Many people find that lying under a weighted blanket helps them feel safe enough to eat. The pressure signals to your brain that you are held, that you are not alone, that you can let your guard down.

What to do when you just can't eat

Sometimes the activation is too strong. Sometimes no amount of grounding or glimmers is going to get you to a place where food feels possible. That's okay. That's not failure. That's information.

In those moments, focus on liquids. Broth. Smoothies. Protein shakes. Something you can drink that doesn't require chewing. Chewing activates the digestive system, but it also requires a certain level of safety. If you can't chew, don't. Just sip. Even a few ounces of something nourishing is better than nothing.

Notice what just happened in your body. Did reading that sentence make you feel a little less alone? I hope so. Because the shame around not being able to eat is often worse than the not eating itself. We tell ourselves we're weak. We tell ourselves we're broken. We tell ourselves everyone else can just eat and why can't we? But that's not true. Everyone has a nervous system. And everyone's nervous system has limits. Yours are just more sensitive. That's not a curse. It's a sign that you've been through something. And that something deserves compassion, not criticism.

Bessel van der Kolk, the author of "The Body Keeps the Score," has spent decades researching how trauma affects the body. He says that the body keeps the score. Your appetite isn't disappearing because you're difficult. It's disappearing because your body is keeping score of every threat it's ever faced. And it's trying to protect you from more. The only way to change that is to show your body, over and over again, that it's safe now. That takes time. That takes patience. That takes a lot of small, gentle moments.

So the next time your appetite disappears, don't panic. Don't force it. Don't shame yourself. Instead, ask your body what it needs. Maybe it needs to move. Maybe it needs to rest. Maybe it needs to be held. Maybe it just needs to know that you're paying attention. Sit with that. Stay with me here. Your body is not your enemy. It's your oldest ally. And it's been trying to keep you alive the only way it knows how.
